---
title: "Exercise: reverse complement on the GPU"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Compute the [reverse complement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complementarity_(molecular_biology)) of an ASCII DNA buffer on the GPU. Each output byte at position `i` is the complement of the input byte at position `n - 1 - i`. Same problem as [day 2 exercise 1](../day2/01-reverse-complement.qmd) — now on the GPU.

By the time `cargo test` passes you will have written a WGSL `switch` on byte values, used a separate output buffer (no in-place reversal), and packed/unpacked bytes through `u32` storage.

## Background

The bio rule: `A↔T`, `C↔G`. Anything else (`N`, lowercase, gaps) should be passed through unchanged (or you can panic — we will pass through).

The shader does one thread per output byte:

```wgsl
@group(0) @binding(0) var<storage, read>       input:  array<u32>;   // packed bytes
@group(0) @binding(1) var<uniform>             info:   Info;         // n_bytes
@group(0) @binding(2) var<storage, read_write> output: array<u32>;   // packed bytes

struct Info { n_bytes: u32, _pad: vec3<u32> }

fn get_byte(arr: ptr<storage, array<u32>, read>, i: u32) -> u32 {
    let word = (*arr)[i / 4u];
    return (word >> ((i & 3u) * 8u)) & 0xffu;
}

fn complement(b: u32) -> u32 {
    switch (b) {
        case 0x41u: { return 0x54u; }            // A -> T
        case 0x43u: { return 0x47u; }            // C -> G
        case 0x47u: { return 0x43u; }            // G -> C
        case 0x54u: { return 0x41u; }            // T -> A
        default:    { return b; }                 // pass-through
    }
}

@compute @workgroup_size(64)
fn main(@builtin(global_invocation_id) gid: vec3<u32>) {
    let i = gid.x;
    if (i >= info.n_bytes) { return; }
    let src_byte = get_byte(&input, info.n_bytes - 1u - i);
    let comp     = complement(src_byte);
    let word_idx = i / 4u;
    let shift    = (i & 3u) * 8u;
    atomicOr(&output_word(word_idx), comp << shift);
    // NOTE: in the real solution we avoid atomicOr by writing
    // u32 words at a time; see hint 3 for the simpler pattern.
}
```

The interesting bits:

- **Reading is straightforward** — `get_byte(&input, src_index)` unpacks any byte.
- **Writing per-byte into a packed `u32` buffer is awkward** — two threads might want to write to the same `u32` word. The cleanest fix is to dispatch one thread per *word*, have each thread compute four output bytes from four read positions, and write the assembled `u32` directly. That's what the solution does.

## The exercise

Open `day12/ex-revcomp/`. The wgpu plumbing is provided. Your job:

1. Decide whether to dispatch by byte (and handle the write collision) or by word (cleaner). The starter scaffolding is the word-dispatch variant.
2. Implement the WGSL `complement` helper.
3. Fill in the body of `main` so it computes four output bytes from four input positions and assembles them into one output `u32`.

Tests check round-trip on random DNA: `revcomp(revcomp(x)) == x`, plus a few hand-crafted comparisons against a CPU reference.

## How to run

```bash
cd day12/ex-revcomp
cargo test
cargo run --release       # reverse-complements a 1 MB random DNA buffer
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — the complement table in WGSL"}
A `switch` on ASCII values is the most readable form. ASCII codes you need: `'A' = 0x41`, `'C' = 0x43`, `'G' = 0x47`, `'T' = 0x54`. The complement maps `A↔T` and `C↔G`; anything else is passed through.

If you prefer a lookup table, you can use a `var<workgroup> table: array<u32, 256>;` initialised by the first thread of each workgroup with a barrier — but for four cases the `switch` is much clearer.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — one thread per output word"}
Dispatch `n_words = (n_bytes + 3) / 4` workgroups' worth of threads. Inside the shader, each thread handles four output bytes:

```wgsl
let out_word = gid.x;
if (out_word >= n_words) { return; }
var assembled: u32 = 0u;
for (var lane: u32 = 0u; lane < 4u; lane = lane + 1u) {
    let out_byte = out_word * 4u + lane;
    if (out_byte < info.n_bytes) {
        let src = info.n_bytes - 1u - out_byte;
        let b   = complement(get_byte(&input, src));
        assembled = assembled | (b << (lane * 8u));
    }
}
output[out_word] = assembled;
```

No atomics needed — each output word is touched by exactly one thread.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — read-back into Vec<u8>"}
The output staging buffer holds `u32`s. To get back to a `Vec<u8>` of the right length you can `bytemuck::cast_slice` and then truncate:

```rust
let words: &[u32] = bytemuck::cast_slice(&mapped);
let mut bytes: Vec<u8> = words.iter()
    .flat_map(|w| w.to_le_bytes())
    .collect();
bytes.truncate(n_bytes);
```

The final truncate drops the padding bytes in the last word.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
See `day12/ex-revcomp-solution/src/main.rs`. The shader uses the "one thread per output word" pattern from hint 2; the host packs / unpacks bytes via `bytemuck`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Side-by-side with the day-2 CPU version**: at small N (≤ 10 KB) the CPU wins by a wide margin (no transfers, no kernel launch). At large N (≥ 10 MB resident on the GPU) the GPU wins by 10× or more.
- **The "one-thread-per-output-word" pattern** generalises: anywhere your output type is smaller than the shader's smallest writable unit (`u32`), dispatch by output-word and let each thread assemble.
- **For a real bioinformatics tool** you'd combine this with the FASTA reader's mmap — keep the genome on the GPU once, then process many windows without transfers.
